title: Slab coaster set in air-dry clay
subtitle: Four square coasters cut from a single rolled slab with impressed surface texture
excerpt: A matching set of four square coasters rolled from a single slab, textured with lace or leaves before cutting, then sealed for everyday use under cool dry items. No kiln required; air-dry clay handles the small flat format well and the texture pass adds visual interest without extra construction. Decorative use only with a felt backing pad to protect surfaces.
sourceNotes: Slab and impressed-texture techniques derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for historical impressed-surface ceramic reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used as the building unit in slab-built work.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim cleanly. Feels firm, like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. For air-dry clay, bone-dry is the finished structural state.

content.text: A set of four square coasters made by rolling one
slab

, pressing a piece of lace or a leaf into the surface, and cutting the slab into four equal squares. No kiln required. The coasters use air-dry clay, which is dense enough to make a stable coaster but porous; they are decorative and need a felt backing pad on the underside to protect wood surfaces from any residual damp.

items.name: 8 mm guide rails
items.substitution: Two stacks of two 4 mm wooden ice-lolly sticks

items.name: Texture piece: lace, leaf, or rubber stamp
Self-adhesive felt pads (10 mm)
items.substitution: Cork sheet cut into 10 mm squares

content.text: Preparation
Wedge the clay for two or three minutes on the board to even the moisture and clear air pockets. A poorly wedged slab cracks across the surface as it dries.
Rolling and texturing
1. Roll the slab

Pat the clay into a thick rectangle on the board. Lay the guide rails on either side of the clay. Roll with the rolling pin riding on the rails: this guarantees an even 8 mm thickness across the slab. Lift and rotate the slab a quarter-turn between passes to keep it from sticking and to even out any thin spots. The finished slab should be about 22 cm by 22 cm.
2. Press the texture

Lay the lace, leaf, or fabric across the slab. Roll once firmly across the texture piece with the pin to press the pattern in. Lift the texture piece straight up; pulling sideways smears the impression. If using a stamp, press straight down with even pressure across the slab. Texture only one side of the slab.
3. Cut the squares

Lay the 95 mm card template on the slab. Cut around it with the fettling knife held straight up, so the edge is square and not bevelled. Mark and cut four squares from the slab, spacing the cuts to give clean edges to each coaster. Set the cut squares on a fresh board.
4. Smooth the cut edges

Run the wooden rib along each cut edge to compress and smooth. Press lightly so the texture on the face is not flattened. The slight bevel a rib leaves on the upper edge softens the look and is preferable to a knife-sharp corner.
Drying

Flat pieces warp as they dry if one face dries faster than the other. Place a sheet of paper between each coaster and the board to slow the underside, and cover loosely with plastic for the first 12 hours. Flip the coasters every six hours so air can reach both faces. The coasters reach
leather-hard
in around 8 to 12 hours; uncover and let them finish to
bone-dry
over a further day or two.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand each edge and the back with 220-grit sandpaper. Brush off the dust. Seal both faces and all four edges with two coats of PVA or acrylic varnish. Let the first coat dry fully before applying the second. The sealer protects the porous clay from absorbing condensation from a cold glass. Stick four felt pads onto the back of each coaster, one in each corner, 5 mm in from the edge.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof. The sealer takes the brunt of any condensation but the coasters are best for cool dry mugs and short use under a damp glass. Wipe with a damp cloth; do not submerge or wash under a running tap. Store flat in a dry place.
Variations
Press a fresh leaf with prominent veins (sage, oak, fig) into the slab for a botanical surface. Pull the leaf away before the clay dries to keep the imprint clean.
Cut round coasters 100 mm in diameter using a cake tin or saucer as a template instead of a square card.
Paint each coaster a different colour after sealing, leaving the textured face plain or accenting the impressed pattern with a wash.

items.symptom: Coasters warp or curl as they dry
items.cause: One face dried faster than the other
items.fix: Cover loosely with plastic for the first 12 hours and flip every 6 hours; place a clean tile or board on top for the first hour to weight them flat

items.symptom: Cracks run across the face of a coaster
items.cause: Clay not wedged enough, or rolled too thin
items.fix: Wedge for a full three minutes next time; keep the slab at 8 mm with the guide rails; cracks already formed can be filled with paper clay slurry before sealing

items.symptom: Texture impression is smeared or shallow
items.cause: Texture piece was pulled sideways instead of lifted straight up, or pressed too lightly
items.fix: Roll firmly once across the texture piece, then lift vertically; press a stamp straight down without rocking
